Content-Security-Policy.explanation: Content Security Policy (CSP) defines what sources of content are allowed to be loaded by the browser. It is the primary defence against Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks by controlling which scripts, styles, images, and other resources can execute.
Content-Security-Policy.attackPrevented: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting), data injection, clickjacking via frames
Content-Security-Policy.example: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-{random}'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:;

X-Content-Type-Options.explanation: Prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing a response away from the declared content-type. Without this header, old browsers might execute a .jpg file as JavaScript if it contains script-like content.
X-Content-Type-Options.attackPrevented: MIME-type sniffing, drive-by downloads, content injection

X-Frame-Options.explanation: Controls whether a browser should be allowed to render a page in a <frame>, <iframe>, <embed>, or <object>. This prevents clickjacking attacks where a malicious site embeds your site in a frame to trick users into clicking on hidden elements.

Strict-Transport-Security.explanation: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) instructs browsers to only access the site over HTTPS for a specified period. Once set, the browser refuses HTTP connections and automatically upgrades to HTTPS, even if the user types 'http://'.
Strict-Transport-Security.attackPrevented: SSL stripping, protocol downgrade attacks, man-in-the-middle attacks

Referrer-Policy.explanation: Controls how much referrer information (sent via the Referer header) should be included with requests. Prevents leaking sensitive URL parameters or paths to third-party services.
Referrer-Policy.attackPrevented: Information disclosure, session token leakage via Referer header

Permissions-Policy.explanation: Allows or denies browser features and APIs (camera, microphone, geolocation, etc.) for the origin. Replaces the older Feature-Policy header. Limits the attack surface by disabling unneeded browser capabilities.
Permissions-Policy.attackPrevented: Unauthorized access to user's camera/mic/location, fingerprinting via FLoC

X-XSS-Protection.explanation: A legacy header that was used to activate the XSS filter built into older browsers (IE, Chrome <78, Safari). Modern browsers have deprecated this in favour of CSP. The OWASP-recommended value is '0' to disable the broken filter, which could itself introduce vulnerabilities.
X-XSS-Protection.attackPrevented: Legacy XSS filter bypass (setting to 0 prevents filter-induced vulnerabilities)

Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy.explanation: COOP isolates browsing context groups. It prevents a malicious website opened in a new tab from gaining access to your global object. Essential for enabling cross-origin isolation and access to high-resolution timers (SharedArrayBuffer).

Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy.explanation: CORP prevents other origins from loading your resources (images, scripts, etc.) via no-cors requests. Defends against cross-site information leaks and Spectre-based side-channel attacks.
Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy.attackPrevented: Spectre side-channel attacks, cross-origin data leakage

Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy.explanation: COEP requires all cross-origin resources to explicitly grant permission to be loaded (via CORS or CORP headers). Required alongside COOP to enable cross-origin isolation (SharedArrayBuffer, high-res timers).
Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy.attackPrevented: Cross-origin data leaks, Spectre mitigations bypass

Server.explanation: Removing the Server header prevents information disclosure about the web server technology and version. Attackers use this to specifically target known vulnerabilities in older server versions.
Server.attackPrevented: Fingerprinting, targeted attacks on specific server software
Server.example: Server header should be removed entirely

A01:2025.name: Broken Access Control
A01:2025.description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of data.
A01:2025.mitigation: Deny access by default. Implement proper RBAC/ABAC. Log and alert on access control failures. Rate limit API and controller access. Invalidate sessions after logout.
A01:2025.prevalence: 94% of applications tested had some form of broken access control

A02:2025.name: Security Misconfiguration
A02:2025.description: Applications are frequently misconfigured: unnecessary features enabled, default accounts unchanged, error handling revealing stack traces, or missing security headers. This is what secure-ref primarily addresses.
A02:2025.mitigation: Use secure-ref to automate OWASP headers. Disable unused features. Segment architecture. Implement automated config validation. Use minimal, hardened base images.
A02:2025.prevalence: 90%+ of Node.js apps lack one or more recommended security headers

A03:2025.name: Software Supply Chain Failures
A03:2025.description: Malicious code injected via compromised npm packages, insecure CI/CD pipelines, or dependency confusion attacks. Examples: event-stream (2018), ua-parser-js (2021), node-ipc (2022).
A03:2025.mitigation: Use zero-dependency packages (like secure-ref). Run npm audit regularly. Enable npm provenance. Pin dependency versions. Use lockfiles. Audit transitive dependencies.
A03:2025.prevalence: Software supply chain attacks grew 742% in 2022 compared to the prior three years combined

A04:2025.name: Insecure Design
A04:2025.description: Missing or ineffective control design — not just implementation bugs. Includes threat modelling failures, insecure architecture decisions, or missing compensating controls.
A04:2025.mitigation: Integrate threat modelling into design phase. Use secure design patterns. Define security user stories. Implement reference architectures. Apply defence-in-depth.
A04:2025.prevalence: A new category for 2021/2025, reflecting the shift to design-level security

A05:2025.name: Security Misconfiguration (Application Level)
A05:2025.description: Improper security configuration at the application layer: verbose error messages, default credentials, unnecessary HTTP methods enabled, missing security headers, overly permissive CORS.
A05:2025.mitigation: Use secure-ref for automatic header hardening. Validate all configuration at startup. Implement environment parity checks. Use security linters.
A05:2025.prevalence: Moved up from #6 in prior OWASP editions

A06:2025.name: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
A06:2025.description: Using components (libraries, frameworks, modules) with known vulnerabilities. Many breaches exploit publicly known vulnerabilities for which patches exist but haven't been applied.
A06:2025.mitigation: Use only what you need (zero-dep philosophy). Subscribe to CVE alerts. Automate dependency updates (Dependabot). Regularly run npm audit. Remove unused dependencies.
A06:2025.prevalence: Log4Shell affected millions of apps using an outdated Log4j version

A07:2025.name: Identification and Authentication Failures
A07:2025.description: Weaknesses in authentication: weak passwords, credential stuffing, missing MFA, insecure session management, JWT manipulation.
A07:2025.mitigation: Use secure-ref JWT helpers (OWASP-enforced standards). Implement MFA. Rate-limit login endpoints. Use secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookies (secureRef.cookie()). Validate all JWTs server-side.
A07:2025.prevalence: Credential stuffing is now automated and used in millions of attacks daily

A08:2025.name: Software and Data Integrity Failures
A08:2025.description: Code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations: deserialization of untrusted data, insecure CI/CD pipelines, auto-update without signature verification.
A08:2025.mitigation: Use SRI (secureRef.sri()) for CDN resources. Verify digital signatures. Use trusted package repositories. Implement CI/CD pipeline security controls. Avoid unsafe deserialization.
A08:2025.prevalence: SolarWinds attack compromised 18,000+ organisations via a tampered software update

A09:2025.name: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
A09:2025.description: Insufficient logging, detection, and response. Organisations cannot detect breaches, investigate incidents, or respond to attacks in progress. Average breach detection time exceeds 200 days.
A09:2025.mitigation: Use secureRef.log() for security event logging. Log all auth failures, access control violations, and suspicious requests. Centralise logs (SIEM). Set up alerting for critical events.
A09:2025.prevalence: Most successful cyberattacks exploit the absence of real-time monitoring

A10:2025.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
A10:2025.description: SSRF flaws occur when a web application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. Attackers can coerce the server to make requests to internal services, cloud metadata APIs, or localhost.
A10:2025.mitigation: Validate and sanitise all client-supplied input URLs. Use an allowlist for remote resources. Disable HTTP redirections. Enforce network-level controls. Never use user input directly in server-side fetch calls.
A10:2025.prevalence: SSRF was used in the Capital One breach (2019) to access AWS metadata endpoint

items: Always use secureRef() middleware before any routes
Customise CSP per route if needed (e.g., stricter on auth pages)
Add Expect-CT header for Certificate Transparency in production
Remove all headers that disclose technology stack (Server, X-Powered-By)
Never disable HSTS on production — once set, removal takes max-age duration
Use secureRef.cookie() for all session cookies
Rotate session IDs after privilege escalation
Implement account lockout after N failed login attempts
Store password hashes with bcrypt (cost factor ≥12) or Argon2id
Never store passwords in plaintext, logs, or environment variables
Validate JWT on every request — never trust client-side claims
Validate ALL inputs server-side regardless of client-side validation
Use parameterised queries / ORM for all database operations
Sanitize HTML output with a library like DOMPurify
Use secureRef.sanitize() for quick XSS stripping in user-generated content
Never render raw user input in HTML templates
Apply secureRef.rateLimit() to all public endpoints
Rate-limit authentication endpoints more strictly (e.g., 5 req/min)
Use API versioning and deprecate old versions quickly
Return 429 with Retry-After header when rate limits are exceeded
Validate Content-Type on all POST/PUT requests
Prefer zero-dependency packages (like secure-ref)
Lock all dependency versions in package-lock.json / yarn.lock
Run npm audit in CI/CD pipeline — fail on high severity
Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated security updates
Use npm provenance to validate package authenticity
Audit transitive (indirect) dependencies with tools like snyk
Always serve over HTTPS — use HSTS preloading for production
Use environment variables for secrets — never hard-code
Rotate credentials and API keys on a schedule
Implement WAF (Web Application Firewall) for production traffic
Use Content Delivery Networks with SRI hashes for CDN assets (secureRef.sri())
Enable npm 2FA and signed commits for package publishing
